coredata.dc:title: In vitro and in vivo highly effective antibacterial activity of carbon dots-modified TiO2 nanorod arrays on titanium
coredata.dc:creator: He, Dongmei
Zhang, Xiangyu
Yao, Xiaohong
Yang, Yongqiang
coredata.dc:description: Light-triggered antibacterial therapy has been proven to be a secure and effective way to treat bacterial infection. Nevertheless, the long-term security of the common photosensitizer remains to be seen in the body. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) with good biocompatibility are incorporated into TiO2 nanorods to improve the photocatalytic and photothermal ability of titanium implants under the irradiation of visible light (VL) and near-infrared (NIR) light. The C-TiO2 NR exhibit excellent in vitro and in vivo antimicrobial effect under 660 nm VL and 808 nm NIR light co-irradiation owing to the combined effect of hyperthermia, reactive oxygen species (ROS) and nanorod structure. Besides, C-TiO2 NR can improve the adhesion and diffusion of bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photo-sensitive coating
Titanium
Antibacterial property
Biocompatibility

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM graphic of CDs; (b-c) SEM images of TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR; (d) FTIR spectrum of different specimens; (e) XPS spectrum of TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR; (f-h) High-resolution XPS spectroscopy of Ti 2p, C 1 s and O 1 s of C-TiO2 NR; (i) XRD spectrum of CDs, TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR; (j) Contact angle on Ti, TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR (***P < 0.001).

(a) Pictures of S. aureus colonies on the surface of Ti, TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR; (b) Antibacterial efficiency of Ti, TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR; (c) Fluorescence images of S. aureus (*p < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001).

Photodynamic and photothermal properties of Ti, TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR under different light sources.(a–c). •OH detected from the degradation of methyl violet; (d–f) 1O2 detected from the degradation of DPBF; (g–i) Photothermal heating curves.

(a) Fluorescence images of BMSCs cultured on Ti, TiO2 NR and C-TiO2 NR for 24 h; (b) Live/dead staining and (c-d) the MTT results of BMSCs after culturing for 1, 3 and 5 days on Ti, TiO2 NR and C-TiO2 NR with or without light.

(a) Photothermal graphics and (b) temperature variation of Ti, TiO2 and C-TiO2 NA under dual light (808 nm NIR + 660 nm VL) irradiation 15 min in vivo; (c) Pictures of S. aureus colonies; (d) Antibacterial efficiency in vivo (**P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001).
Giemsa and H&E staining pictures of the tissues around the implantation site.
H&E staining pictures of the heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney tissue slices after 14 days of feeding.

head.ce:title: In vitro and in vivo highly effective antibacterial activity of carbon dots-modified TiO2 nanorod arrays on titanium

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Biomaterial Surfaces & Interfaces, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Light-triggered antibacterial therapy has been proven to be a secure and effective way to treat bacterial infection. Nevertheless, the long-term security of the common photosensitizer remains to be seen in the body. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) with good biocompatibility are incorporated into TiO2 nanorods to improve the photocatalytic and photothermal ability of titanium implants under the irradiation of visible light (VL) and near-infrared (NIR) light. The C-TiO2 NR exhibit excellent in vitro and in vivo antimicrobial effect under 660 nm VL and 808 nm NIR light co-irradiation owing to the combined effect of hyperthermia, reactive oxygen species (ROS) and nanorod structure. Besides, C-TiO2 NR can improve the adhesion and diffusion of bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs).

ce:section.ce:para: Titanium-based alloys have attracted much attention in orthopedic and dental implants owing to their outstanding corrosion resistance, biocompatibility, and mechanical property [1,2]. However, implant-related infection induced by bacteria has become an insurmountable complication [3,4]. Since natural titanium (Ti) has no antibacterial properties, researchers have developed antibacterial surfaces containing antibacterial drugs like antibiotics and metal ions on implants [5–7]. Nevertheless, the massive use or misuse of antibiotics has resulted in increasing bacterial resistance, even the emergence of superbugs [8,9], and the toxicity of the antibacterial metal ions hampers their clinical application [10,11]. Therefore, new strategies need to be developed to endow the surface of Ti with antibacterial activity.

Light-mediated strategies for treating bacteria, including photodynamic and photothermal therapy, have widely used in the antimicrobial field due to the higher antibacterial activity and little side effects [12–16]. Although phototherapy has made great achievements, the long-term safety of common photosensitizer such as ZnPc and porphyrin [17,18] remains to be seen in the body. Titanium dioxide (TiO2), as an inert and stable photocatalyst, has been widely used in biomedical engineering because of its good biocompatibility and photocatalytic activity [19]. Nevertheless, TiO2 can only be activated by ultraviolet light, limiting the application as a photosensitizer for clinical application [20].

Carbon dots (CDs) have received wide attention due to the biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and efficient electron transfer [21–24]. Compared with conventional fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots, CDs mainly consist of sp2 carbon which can limit the recombination of electron -hole, thereby improving the photocatalytic effect [25]. In addition, CDs can absorb near-infrared (NIR) light and is commonly used in photothermal therapy [26,27].

In this work, CDs-doped TiO2 nanorod array (C-TiO2 NR) is fabricated on Ti implant by hydrothermal treatment for highly efficient bacteria killing under co-illumination of 660 nm visible light (VL) and 808 nm near-infrared (NIR) light. Meanwhile, the antibacterial system has good safety. Biocompatible TiO2 nanorod array (TiO2 NR) is firstly prepared on Ti, then CDs are doped into TiO2 nanorods to improve the photothermal and photocatalytic performance under the irradiation of 808 nm and 660 nm light, respectively. TiO2 NR exhibits excellent in vitro and in vivo antimicrobial effect against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) upon irradiation with dual light in a short time owing to the combined effect of photodynamic and photothermal therapy. Besides, C-TiO2 NR promotes the adhesion and spreading of bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs).

L-tryptophan and chlorhexidine acetate were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCL) was obtained from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Absolute ethanol were purchased from Tianjin Bohuatong Chemical Products Sales Center. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin, streptomycin, acridine orange (AO), propidium iodide (PI) and methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) were purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Development Co., Ltd. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), Methyl violet (MV) and 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent company. Glutaraldehyde was obtained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Acetone was supplied by Tianjin Shentai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents were not further purified and the water used for the experiments was deionized water.

1.2 g of both l-tryptophan and chlorhexidine acetate were added to a beaker containing 35 mL of deionized water. After stirring at room temperature for 120 min, the mixture was moved to a 50 mL reaction vessel followed by hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 12 h. The reacted solution was filtered by a 0.22 µm filter to remove unreacted substances and then dialyzed through a 1000 molecular weight dialysis bag for about 2 days. Finally, the resulting CDs solution was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C.

A 2.7 × 3.0 cm2 biomedical Ti foil was selected as the substrate. The specimens were ultrasonically washed in acetone, alcohol and deionized water, respectively. The samples were put into a reaction kettle with 40 mL of 1 mol/L NaOH solution followed by hydrothermal reaction at 220 °C for 4 h. Afterwards, the samples were ultrasonicated for 60 s and soaked in 0.5 mol/L HCl for 30 min. Subsequently, the specimens were washed with deionized water and put into a muffle furnace. After annealed at 550 °C for 4 h, TiO2 NR was prepared.

50 mg of CDs were added to a mixture of 20 mL of deionized water and 10 mL of alcohol. Then, the mixture was ultrasonicated for 10 min and stirred for 60 min to make CDs uniformly disperse. After that, the solution was poured into a 50 mL reaction kettle. Next, the prepared TiO2 NR was put into the reaction kettle. After being treated for 4 h at 140 °C, the samples were washed with deionized water and dried at room temperature. The obtained samples were named as C-TiO2 NR.

CDs were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL). The morphology of C-TiO2 NR was observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM-7001F, JEOL). The surface functional groups of C-TiO2 NR were determined by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, ALPHA II, Bruker, Germany). The chemical states of C-TiO2 NR were analyzed with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo). The phase composition of the samples was determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Dmax-3C, Cu Kα radiation). The surface hydrophilicity of C-TiO2 NR was measured by contact angle instrument (JC2000D2, Powereach, China).

The photodynamic properties of C-TiO2 NR were evaluated with methyl violet (MV) and 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF). The specimens were soaked in 3 mL MV and DPBF, and after exposed to 808 nm NIR light (0.6 W cm−2), 660 nm VL (0.6 W cm−2) and dual light (808 nm, 0.6 W cm−2; 660 nm, 0.6 W cm−2) for 15 min, respectively, the concentration of . OH and 1O2 generated from the samples were detected by measuring the optical density of the solutions with ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometry.

In order to assess the photothermal effect, Ti, TiO2 NR and C-TiO2 NR were soaked in 1 mL PBS and then illuminated with 808 nm NIR light (0.6 W cm−2), 660 nm VL (0.6 W cm−2) and dual light (808 nm, 0.6 W cm−2; 660 nm. 0.6 W cm−2) for 15 min. The real-time temperature of the specimens was determined with an infrared thermal imager (FLIRC 7200, FLIR, USA).

The antibacterial activity of the specimens against S. aureus (ATCC 25923) was evaluated. The specimens were disinfected with 75% sterilized alcohol. Then the sample were placed in 24-well plates. 1 mL of bacterial liquid with a concentration of 1 × 105 CFU mL−1 was inoculated on different samples, and then illuminated with 808 nm NIR light (0.6 W cm−2), 660 nm VL (0.6 W cm−2) and dual light (808 nm, 0.6 W cm−2; 660 nm, 0.6 W cm−2) for 15 min, respectively. At the same time, the temperature of the samples was controlled below 50 °C. After that, the bacterial liquid was diluted to 103 with PBS and 80 µL of diluted the bacterial liquid was spread on agar plates. After culturing for 24 h at 37 °C, the bacterial colonies were observed and counted. The antimicrobial efficiency was computed using the following equation: antibacterial efficiency (%) = (NC - NS)/NC.
NC is the average number of bacterial colonies on the control specimen (unirradiated Ti group) and NS is the average number of bacterial colonies on Ti after irradiation.

The antimicrobial effect of Ti, TiO2 NR and C-TiO2 NR was further evaluated by live/dead fluorescent staining. 2 mL of bacterial liquid (1 × 105 CFU mL−1) was transferred to the different specimens. The samples were illuminated with dual light (808 nm, 0.6 W cm−2; 660 nm, 0.6 W cm−2) for 15 min. Subsequently, the samples were stained by acridine orange/propidium iodide for 10 min in darkness. Finally, three areas were randomly observed and photographed with a fluorescence microscope.

Bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) were extracted from the femoral and tibial bone marrow of male SD mice. 3 mL of α-MEM medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin/streptomycin were added into the cell flask. The extracted BMSCs were placed in the cell flask and cultured in a cell culture incubator containing 5% CO2. The medium was changed once a day. When the cells were fully adherent and have reached 80~90% fusion, the cell suspension was inoculated onto the surface of Ti, TiO2 NR and C-TiO2 NR. All samples were disinfected with 75% sterile alcohol and washed three times with PBS, and finally placed in 24-well plates.

Cell morphology: 1 mL of cell suspension (2 × 104 cells/cm2) was inoculated onto the different specimens. After incubation for 24 h, the cells were fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde for 40 min. After that, the cells were stained by FITC for 40 min and DAPI for 15 min in darkness, respectively. The morphology of cells was observed and photographed with a laser confocal microscope.

Cell viability assay: The cell viability on Ti, TiO2 NR and C-TiO2 NR was assessed by live/dead fluorescent staining (calcein AM and EthD-1) and MTT (Sigma, USA). 1 mL of cell suspension (1 × 104 cells/cm2) was inoculated onto the different specimens. After 1, 3 and 5 days of incubation, 50 µL of staining solution was introduced to the surface of samples in the dark and incubated for 40 min. After that, the viability of the cells was observed by laser confocal microscopy. Cell viability was assessed by MTT on different samples under light (light +) and no light (light -) irradiation conditions. For the light group, cells on the first day samples were irradiated with dual light (808 nm, 0.6 W cm−2; 660 nm, 0.6 W cm−2) for 15 min and the temperature was controlled to below 50 °. After 1, 3 and 5 days of incubation, all samples were transferred into a new well, then 900 µL medium and 100 µL MTT were added. After incubation for 4 h in the dark, the solution was discarded. Subsequently, 1 mL DMSO was added and the optical density of the solution was recorded at 492 nm.

A subcutaneous model of S. aureus infection was established in rats, and Ti, TiO2 NR and C-TiO2 NR were implanted into the desired sites to assess their antimicrobial properties in vivo. Male rats (300−310) were bought from Shanxi Medical University. In addition, the animal experiments were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of “Taiyuan University of technology”. The rats were randomly divided into Ti, TiO2 NR, and C-TiO2 NR groups, with six mice in each group. The mice were anesthetized with anesthetic, and the subcutaneous area was debrided. A 15 mm wound was created at the site with a scalpel, then 50 µL of S. aureus (1 × 105 CFU mL−1) was dropped and soaked for 5 min to establish a bacterial infection model. A specimen (10 mm length and 5 mm wide) was cleaned by 75% sterile alcohol and PBS was implanted into the wound. After seamed, the implantation site was exposed to dual light (808 nm, 0.6 W cm−2; 660 nm, 0.6 W cm−2) for 15 min, and the maximum temperature was controlled below 50 °C. After two days of feeding, three mice in each group were euthanized. Then, the samples were taken out and soaked in 1 mL of PBS. After sonicated to evenly disperse the bacteria in PBS, 80 µL of the bacterial liquid was inoculated onto standard medium and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Meanwhile, the tissues around the implants were obtained and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde. And the histomorphological changes of issues were observed with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining and Giemsa staining. After 14 days of feeding, the heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney of rats were also examined by H&E staining to assess their safety in vivo.

All experiments were repeated three times and data were showed as mean ± standard deviation (SD) by SPSS 14.0 software. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way ANOVA. (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001 are regarded as significant, highly significant and extremely significant, respectively).

CDs prepared by the hydrothermal method are analyzed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a, the average size of CDs is about 5 nm. The lattice distance of 0.26 nm corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [28]. The TiO2 NR on Ti was also prepared by the hydrothermal method in NaOH solution, as described previously [29,30], and the surface topography is shown in Fig. 1b. A nanorod array structure with a mean diameter of about 45 nm is fabricated on Ti. CDs are doped into TiO2 NR by the hydrothermal treatment again in a CDs-containing solution. The diameter of the C-TiO2 nanorod increases to 60 nm (Fig. 1c) due to the increase of the hydrothermal time [31].

The FTIR spectroscopy of different samples are shown in Fig. 1d. The major absorption band below 1000 cm−1 is assigned to TiO2 NR characteristic absorption [32]. The absorption peaks of CDs at 3308 cm−1, 2492 cm−1, 1615 cm−1, 1461 cm−1 and 1230 cm−1 are assigned to O-H/N-H, C-H, CO, C-N and C-O stretching vibration, respectively [33]. These absorption characteristics are also found in the spectra of C-TiO2 NR, indicting that CDs are successfully incorporated into TiO2 nanorod.

XPS is also utilized to confirm the chemical states of C-TiO2 and as shown in the survey scan (Fig. 1e), apart from C, Ti and O, N is also detected from C-TiO2 NR. Fig. 1f illustrates the high-resolution spectrum of Ti 2p in C-TiO2 NR, showing that Ti is present as TiO2 phase. The C 1 s peaks (Fig. 1g) of C-TiO2 NR are at 284.2 eV, 286.3 eV and 288.6 eV corresponding to CC, C-O, and CO, respectively [34]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1h) can be fitted as three peaks at 530.7 eV, 532.5 eV and 534.4 eV, which are attributed to Ti-O, C-O and O-H, respectively [35]. These results further indicate that the TiO2 NR is successfully modified with CDs.

Fig. 1i shows the XRD patterns of CD, TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR. A broad peak in the pattern of CDs at 20° can be well assigned to the (100) planes of graphitic carbon, being consistent with the results previously reported [36]. However, the characteristic diffraction peaks of CDs can not be observed in the pattern of C-TiO2, and there are no obvious differences in phases between the TiO2 and C-TiO2. This can be accounted for the fact that the concentration of CDs in the CDs is below the detection limit of the XRD analysis.

The water contact angles of different samples are illustrated in Fig. 1j. Compared with Ti, the contact angle of TiO2 NR decreases to 9.16°. After the doping of CDs, the contact angle further reduces to 7.19° because the surface of CDs contains abundant hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups [37].

The antimicrobial property of Ti, TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR against S. aureus under 808 nm, 660 nm and dual light irradiation is assessed by the spread plate method, and the results are shown in Fig. 2a-b. After single or double light illumination for 15 min, the S. aureus colonies cover nearly the whole agar plates corresponding to Ti, and the antibacterial efficiency of Ti under various light irradiation is less than 20%. After the hydrothermal treatment, the antibacterial activity of TiO2 NR is improved slightly under various light illumination, and the antibacterial efficiency under dual light irradiation reaches about 50%. However, the doping of CDs significantly improves the antibacterial activity of TiO2 NR, and the antibacterial efficiency of C-TiO2 NR under 808 nm, 660 nm and dual light irradiation is about 32%, 30% and 99.9%, respectively.

The antibacterial property of different samples is also determined by fluorescence staining. As shown in Fig. 2c, upon illumination with dual light for 15 min, green fluorescence spots are observed on the Ti surface. In contrast, lots of red fluorescence spots are found on the surface of TiO2 NR, indicating that lots of bacteria are dead. As for C-TiO2 NR, almost all bacteria are stained red, suggesting the excellent antibacterial activity of C-TiO2 NR under dual light irradiation.

The photodynamic property of Ti, TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR under different light sources is firstly investigated. The yield of . OH and 1O2 generated by the specimens is examined using MV and DPBF. . OH and 1O2 can react with MV and DPBF respectively, leading to a decrease in the absorption value at 580 nm and 420 nm [38,39]. Thus, the decrease of absorbance value can reflect the yield of .OH and 1O2. As shown in Fig. 3a-c, no matter which light irradiation, the intensity of the absorption peaks of TiO2 NR is slightly lower than that of Ti, which can be attributed to the absorption of MV solution by the nanostructure. Under 808 nm light irradiation, the intensity of the absorption peaks of C-TiO2 NR is basically the same as TiO2 NR, indicating that 808 nm light cannot motivate . OH form C-TiO2 NR. After irradiation with 660 nm light for 15 min, the corresponding absorption intensity values of C-TiO2 NR decreases clearly, meaning that the photocatalytic effect of C-TiO2 NR under 660 nm light irradiation is improved significantly. Under dual light irradiation, the absorption intensity values of various samples is essentially the same as that of under 660 nm light irradiation, further suggesting that only 660 nm light can inspire . OH form C-TiO2 NR. The production of 1O2 is similar to that of .OH. As shown in Fig. 3d-f, C-TiO2 can not be excited to produce 1O2 under 808 nm light irradiation, while it can generate 1O2 under 660 nm and dual light irradiation.

CDs are mainly composed of sp2 carbon which can undergo interfacial charge transfer with TiO2, thus enhancing photocatalytic performance [40]. Under the excitation of light, electrons on the HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) of CDs are inspired to the LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) and subsequently transferred to the CB of TiO2, thus leaving holes on the valence band of CDs. These holes can react with OH- to form hydroxyl radicals [41].

The photothermal property of different samples under 808 nm light, 660 nm and dual light irradiation is investigated, and the results are illustrated in Fig. 3g-i. All samples exhibit better photothermal conversion ability under 808 nm light irradiation than that of under 660 nm irradiation, and under the irradiation of dual light, the temperature of Ti, TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR goes up further. No matter what the light irradiation, the temperature of TiO2 NR is higher than that of pure Ti, which can be attributed to the optical property of the TiO2 nanostructure [29] . The doping of CDs further improves the photothermal property of TiO2 NR, and the temperature of C-TiO2 NR reaches to 50 ℃ under dual light irradiation for 15 min

In addition to the generated ROS and hyperthermia under light irradiation, the antibacterial activity of the implants is also related to the nanostructures of the surface. In our previous work, we have confirmed that the nanorod structure of TiO2 played a significant role in killing bacteria [29]. Therefore, the combined effect of ROS, hyperthermia and puncturing through TiO2 nanorods make the Ti implant possess excellent antibacterial activity.

The F-actin and nuclei of BMSCs cultured on Ti, TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR for 24 h are stained, and the results are shown in Fig. 4a. Most of the BMSCs are a long strip and spread poorly on Ti after culturing for 24 h. In contrast, the cells on TiO2 NR have a polygonal shape and the spreading areas increase. This is because nanostructures can enhance cell-biomaterial interactions by activating integrin-mediated signaling pathways, thus enhancing cell adhesion and spreading [42]. However, the spreading of cells on C-TiO2 NR is further improved, suggesting that CDs are beneficial for adhesion and growth of BMSCs [43].

The biocompatibility of Ti, TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR is assessed by live/dead fluorescent staining and the results are shown in Fig. 4b. No dead cells are observed on all samples and the number of BMSCs increases with increasing culture time, indicating that C-TiO2 NR has good biocompatibility. The cytotoxicity of the different samples is also assessed using MTT. As shown in Figs. 4c and 4d, there is a linear increase in the number of BMSCs on various samples on day 5 with or without light irradiation. Additionally, TiO2 NR promotes cell proliferation and compared to TiO2 NR, the biocompatibility is further increased on the surface of C-TiO2 NR, which is attributed to the presence of hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs. The hydrophilicity can provide a good microenvironment for cell adhesion and proliferation, being consistent with the results previously reported [44,45]. Meanwhile, light irradiation has an adverse effect on the cell viability of TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR due to the generated hyperthermia and ROS. However, the harm is temporary and the cell viability can rejuvenate when the light is removed.

A subcutaneous model of S. aureus infection is established in rats, and Ti, TiO2 and C-TiO2 NR are implanted into the infected site to assess their antimicrobial activity in vivo. The implantation site is irradiated with dual light for 15 min, and the maximum temperature is kept at less 50 °C. The temperature change in vivo are recorded by heat imager. The results are coincident with those of in vitro, and C-TiO2 NR possesses the best photothermal conversion ability ( Fig. 5a-b).

After feeding for two days, the samples are taken out and the antimicrobial property is firstly evaluated using the spread plate method. The results are shown in Fig. 5c-d, C-TiO2 NR exhibits good in vivo antimicrobial activity, and the antibacterial efficiency reaches 85%. H&E staining and Giemsa staining are also used to assess the inflammation and bacterial infection in the soft tissues around the implants. The results are shown in Fig. 6, the presence of a lot of immune cells and bacteria (labeled with red arrows) in the control group Ti suggest the acute inflammation. In contrast, the number of immune cells and bacteria are reducing in the TiO2 NR group. As for C-TiO2 NR group, fewer inflammatory cells and bacteria are seen in the tissues, indicating that the C-TiO2 NR under the irradiation of dual light can prevent bacterial infection in vivo.

To confirm the biological safety of C-TiO2 NR in vivo, the changes of major organs in the remaining rats are observed by H&E staining after culturing for 14 days. As shown in Fig. 7, no significant abnormalities can be observed in the main organs, proving that the side effects of the light treatment system on the rats can be neglected.

In summary, a good biocompatible C-TiO2 NR is prepared on Ti to combat bacterial infections. C-TiO2 NR exhibits excellent photothermal and photocatalytic activity under the irradiation of 808 nm NIR and 660 nm VL light, respectively. Under the irradiation of dual light, the NR have excellent in vitro and in vivo antibacterial activity owing to the combined effect of hyperthermia, ROS and nanorod structure. Moreover, C-TiO2 NR can facilitate the adhesion and diffusion of BMSCs. Hence, this light-assisted antibacterial therapy is regarded as a secure and effective way to prevent bacterial infection in clinical applications.

Dongmei He: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization; Xiangyu Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration; Xiaohong Yao: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis; Yongqiang Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52171240 and 31700834), Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (2021M691992), Major Projects in Research and Development of Shanxi (Projects of International Cooperation, 201803D421090) and Natural Science Foundation of Special Equipment Safety Supervision Inspection Institute of Jiangsu Province(KJ(Y)2020037).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bauer, S.; Schmuki, P.; Mark, K.V. D.; Park, J. Engineering Biocompatible Implant Surfaces. Prog. Mater. Sci. 2013, 58, 261–326.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanostructured titanium-based materials for medical implants: modeling and development

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mishnaevsky, L., Jr.; Levashov, E.; Valiev, R.Z.; Segurado, J.; Sabirov, I.; Enikeev, N.; Prokoshkin, S.; Solov'yov, A.V.; Korotitskiy, A.; Gutmanas, E.; Gotman, I.; Rabkin, E.; Psakh'e, S.; Dluhos, L.; Seefeldt, M.; Smolin, A. Nanostructured Titanium-based Materials for Medical Implants: Modeling and Development. Mater. Sci. Eng. R. 2014, 81, 1–19.
